ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for roasted pork belly as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared pork dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted pork belly is approximately 150g, equivalent to 3–4 slices, as commonly served in a Singaporean hawker or restaurant meal.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Pork Belly — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for roasted pork belly (CR ID: F06100039). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated for very low carbohydrate protein-fat food; analogous reference: pure protein and fat foods, University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted pork belly contains only 0.9g carbohydrate per 100g. No direct GI measurement exists for this food in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 10 (low) based on the principle that foods with negligible carbohydrate content produce minimal postprandial glucose response; this is consistent with analogous high-protein, high-fat foods in the University of Sydney GI Database which register GI values below 15. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (10 × 0.9 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.135, rounded to 0.1 — clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 443mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold; saturated fat estimated at ~11.5g/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk associated with hypertension.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit roasted pork belly to small portions (≤50g) and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or hoisin sauce, as a 150g serving delivers approximately 665mg sodium — over 40% of the recommended daily limit of 1500mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.9g/100g, posing no direct glycaemic impact; however, high saturated fat (~11.5g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted pork belly is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in small portions due to negligible carbohydrate content, but regular consumption should be limited given the high saturated fat load which can impair insulin sensitivity; avoid sweet marinades or glazes that add sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat at 31.9g/100g with estimated saturated fat of ~11.5g/100g significantly exceeds AHA thresholds; combined with 443mg/100g sodium, this food presents a high cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat roasted pork belly as an occasional food only, limiting portions to 50g or less, removing the skin to reduce saturated fat intake, and avoiding high-sodium accompaniments to minimise compounded cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5g/100g) and elevated sodium (443mg/100g) both exceed RA dietary thresholds; pork belly's arachidonic acid content amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit roasted pork belly due to its high saturated fat and arachidonic acid content, which promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory responses; if consumed, limit to 30–40g and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens or turmeric.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted pork belly is a popular dish in Singaporean and Chinese cuisine, prepared by marinating pork belly cuts with a blend of spices and seasonings before roasting at high heat to achieve a crispy outer skin and tender, juicy interior layers of meat and fat. The dish is characterised by its rich, savoury flavour, pronounced umami depth from the marinade, and a satisfying contrast between the crackling skin and the soft, fatty meat beneath. It is commonly served at hawker centres, Chinese restaurants, and family meals across Singapore, often accompanied by rice, steamed vegetables, or dipping sauces. Nutritionally, roasted pork belly is energy-dense at 387 kcal per 100g, with high total fat (31.9g) including an estimated 11.5g saturated fat, and a substantial protein content of 23.9g. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.9g, resulting in a very low glycemic index (estimated 10) and a clinically negligible glycemic load. Sodium is elevated at 443mg/100g due to the spiced marinade. Clinically, this food is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat levels, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to saturated fat's effect on insulin resistance. Portion control and infrequent consumption are recommended for individuals with cardiometabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06100039) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. Saturated fat (11.5g) and sugar (0.5g) are estimated from standard pork belly composition references consistent with HPB data; these secondary values are clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate (low, 10) based on negligible carbohydrate content.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific roasted pork belly preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06100039) as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at ~11.5g/100g based on standard pork belly fatty acid distribution from USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 10006, Pork, fresh, belly, raw) adjusted for roasting. Sugar estimated at ~0.5g/100g from marinade contribution. Dietary fibre is confirmed zero for this meat-based preparation with no plant fibre components. Glycemic index is estimated at 10 (low) as an analogous estimate for this very low carbohydrate food (0.9g/100g); no direct GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for roasted pork belly. Glycemic load per 150g serving is 0.1 — clinically negligible. Halal status is false as pork is not halal.
